topics.topic_name: Structure 1: Models of the particulate nature of matter
out_of_scope: nuclear physics beyond mass spectra and isotopes
quantum mechanical orbital details beyond syllabus
real gas equations beyond ideal gas law
pre-DP descriptive chemistry without quantitative link
classification_hints: question involves particles, atoms, moles, gas laws, or electron arrangement
common_misconceptions: electrons orbit the nucleus like planets
gases have no mass
mole is only a counting unit with no mass link

common_misclassifications.reason: both use models; Structure 1 focuses on particles/atoms/moles, Structure 2 on bonding and materials

topics.topic_name: Introduction to the particulate nature of matter
scope: states of matter and particle arrangement
classification_hints: question asks about states of matter, particle arrangement, or separation techniques
common_misconceptions: particles expand when heated rather than moving further apart

common_misclassifications.reason: both may discuss ionic substances; S1.1 focuses on state/arrangement, S2.1 on ionic bonding model

classification_hints: question asks for subatomic particles, isotopes, or mass spectrum interpretation
common_misconceptions: isotopes have different chemical properties because mass differs

common_misclassifications.reason: both deal with atomic structure; S1.2 focuses on nucleus and isotopes, S1.3 on electron configuration

scope: convergence limit and successive ionization energy (AHL)
out_of_scope: atomic orbitals beyond s/p/d introduction at AHL
classification_hints: question asks for electron configuration, ionization energy, or emission spectrum
common_misconceptions: 4s fills after 3d in all ions
ionization energy decreases down a group only because atomic radius increases

common_misclassifications.reason: both use periodic trends; S1.3 focuses on electron structure, S3.1 on periodic classification

topics.topic_name: Counting particles by mass: The mole
scope: stoichiometric mass and gas volume calculations
out_of_scope: solution concentration is covered in Reactivity 2.1
classification_hints: question requires mole calculations, formula determination, or mass-to-particle conversion
common_misconceptions: molar mass has units of grams rather than g mol^-1
empirical formula is always the same as molecular formula

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve quantitative chemistry; R2.1 focuses on solution stoichiometry and titrations

out_of_scope: real gas equations such as van der Waals
classification_hints: question involves gas laws, ideal gas equation, or molar volume
common_misconceptions: volume is directly proportional to temperature on Celsius scale

common_misclassifications.reason: both may use kinetic theory; S1.5 focuses on gas laws, R2.2 on reaction rates

topics.topic_name: Structure 2: Models of bonding and structure
classification_hints: question asks about bonding, structure, shape, or properties of substances
common_misconceptions: ionic bonds form molecules in the solid state
intermolecular forces are bonds within molecules

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve organic compounds; S2 focuses on bonding/structure, S3.2 on functional-group classification

classification_hints: question asks about ionic bonding, lattice, or properties of ionic compounds
common_misconceptions: ionic compounds form molecules

common_misclassifications.reason: both discuss electron sharing/transfer; S2.1 is electron transfer to ions, S2.2 is electron sharing in covalent bonds

classification_hints: question asks for Lewis structures, molecular shape, bond polarity, or VSEPR
common_misconceptions: lone pairs do not affect molecular shape
all molecules with polar bonds are polar

common_misclassifications.reason: both relate to bonding models; S2.2 is covalent, S2.3 is metallic

classification_hints: question asks about metallic bonding, conductivity, malleability, or alloys
common_misconceptions: metals contain positive ions and negative electrons in fixed positions

common_misclassifications.reason: both discuss lattices of positive ions; S2.3 has delocalized electrons, not discrete anions

classification_hints: question asks about melting/boiling points, solubility, or IMF explanations
common_misconceptions: hydrogen bonding is a covalent bond
stronger IMF always means higher density

common_misclassifications.reason: both explain properties; S2.4 focuses on intermolecular forces, S2.2 on intramolecular covalent bonding

classification_hints: question involves periodic trends, element classification, or organic functional groups
common_misconceptions: atomic radius increases across a period
organic compounds always contain oxygen

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve reactions; Structure 3 classifies substances, Reactivity 3 explains mechanisms of change

topics.topic_name: The periodic table: Classification of elements
scope: periodic trends: atomic radius, ionization energy, electronegativity
out_of_scope: full descriptive chemistry of d-block elements
classification_hints: question asks for periodic trend explanation or group comparisons
common_misconceptions: ionization energy always decreases down a group without exceptions

common_misclassifications.reason: both use ionization energy; S3.1 links it to periodic trends, S1.3 to electron configuration

topics.topic_name: Functional groups: Classification of organic compounds
out_of_scope: reaction mechanisms beyond those in Reactivity 3.4
industrial synthesis routes not listed in syllabus
classification_hints: question asks to name, classify, or identify organic compounds using functional groups or spectra
common_misconceptions: all compounds with C=C decolourize bromine water is the only test
isomers always have different physical properties

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve organic chemistry; S3.2 classifies/identifies, R3.4 explains reaction mechanisms

classification_hints: question asks why reactions occur, enthalpy, or energy cycles
common_misconceptions: exothermic reactions have negative activation energy
bond breaking releases energy

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve quantitative reaction chemistry; R1 focuses on energy, R2 on amount/rate/extent

out_of_scope: correction for heat loss beyond syllabus
classification_hints: question asks to calculate enthalpy change from calorimetry data
common_misconceptions: temperature change alone equals enthalpy change

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve enthalpy; R1.1 is experimental measurement, R1.2 is energy cycles

classification_hints: question requires constructing or using an energy cycle
common_misconceptions: Hess's law only works for two-step cycles

common_misclassifications.reason: both use enthalpy values; R1.2 uses tabulated/cycle values, R1.1 uses experimental data

classification_hints: question compares fuels or discusses energy sources
common_misconceptions: a higher molar enthalpy of combustion always means a better fuel

common_misclassifications.reason: both use combustion enthalpies; R1.3 focuses on fuel context, R1.2 on cycles

classification_hints: question asks about entropy, Gibbs energy, or reaction spontaneity
common_misconceptions: exothermic reactions are always spontaneous
entropy always increases in every system

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve energy; R1.4 uses Gibbs/entropy, R1.2 uses enthalpy cycles

topics.topic_name: Reactivity 2: How much, how fast and how far?
out_of_scope: industrial process design beyond Haber/contact process context
classification_hints: question involves moles in solution, titration, rate, or equilibrium
common_misconceptions: equilibrium means equal concentrations
catalyst shifts equilibrium position

common_misclassifications.reason: both use quantitative data; R2 focuses on amount/rate/extent, R1 on energy

topics.topic_name: How much? The amount of chemical change
scope: molar volume of gases in reactions
classification_hints: question asks for concentration, limiting reactant, yield, or atom economy
common_misconceptions: limiting reactant is the one with smaller mass

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve mole calculations; R2.1 focuses on reactions in solution, S1.4 on formula/mass counting

topics.topic_name: How fast? The rate of chemical change
out_of_scope: full integrated rate laws beyond syllabus
classification_hints: question asks about reaction rate, collision theory, or rate equation
common_misconceptions: increasing temperature only increases collision frequency

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve reaction progress; R2.2 is rate, R2.3 is equilibrium extent

topics.topic_name: How far? The extent of chemical change
classification_hints: question asks about equilibrium constant, Le Chatelier, pH, or buffer
common_misconceptions: catalyst changes the value of Kc
pH + pOH = 14 at all temperatures

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve acids and bases; R2.3 focuses on equilibrium extent, R3.1 on proton transfer reactions

topics.topic_name: Reactivity 3: What are the mechanisms of chemical change?
classification_hints: question asks for reaction type, mechanism, or oxidizing/reducing agents
common_misconceptions: all substitution reactions are SN2
oxidation always involves oxygen

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve organic compounds; R3 explains how they react, S3.2 classifies them

scope: pH of strong and weak acids/bases
out_of_scope: full acid-base titration curves beyond syllabus
classification_hints: question asks about acid-base reactions, pH, neutralization, or buffer
common_misconceptions: strong acids have the lowest pH values at all concentrations
neutralization always gives pH 7

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve acids/bases and equilibrium; R3.1 focuses on proton transfer reactions, R2.3 on extent of change

classification_hints: question asks for oxidation number, redox equation, or electrochemical cell
common_misconceptions: oxidation number is the same as ionic charge
electrolysis always produces the same products regardless of conditions

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve ionic equations; R3.2 is electron transfer, R3.1 is proton transfer

classification_hints: question mentions radicals, UV light, or chain mechanism
common_misconceptions: radicals are ions with an extra electron

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve reaction mechanisms; R3.3 is radical/electron sharing, R3.4 is electron-pair sharing

classification_hints: question asks for organic mechanism, nucleophile/electrophile, or reaction type
common_misconceptions: all nucleophiles are negatively charged
elimination and substitution are mutually exclusive

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve organic chemistry; R3.4 focuses on mechanisms, S3.2 on classification/identification
